essence: OMI list ingestion is too thin — David wants ontology/tags/synopsis metadata surfaced per item plus a sequential A00-style primary-key code so agents can be told 'go get item A001'.

actions.text: Add ontology, tagging, and synopsis metadata (with a detail toggle) to items in the OMI list view
Introduce a sequential primary-key code scheme (A00–A99 → B00... pattern, sized for volume) for OMI list items

loop.label: OMI list metadata + sequential item codes

essence: The five extensions built in the batch are all too thin — minimum viable data with no search guidance, inferred metadata, summaries, usage docs, or discovery UX — and each must be re-evaluated from how a human AND an agent would actually use it.

actions.text: Re-evaluate each of the five batch-built extensions from the perspective of real human and agent usage, adding search, inferred metadata, summaries, and documentation

loop.label: extension discoverability and usage UX depth

essence: Skill registry design: track ~1100 investigated + ~100 in-use skills with last-accessed/staleness, per-skill learnings metadata, rich search (name, description, taxonomy) and a detail viewer with high-quality browsing UX.

actions.text: Design skill registry data structures covering last-accessed, staleness, and captured learnings per skill
Build dynamic search/filter (name, description, taxonomy, type-ahead) plus a skill detail viewer

essence: Long noisy cafe meetup session where David and peers debug a demo app — swapping API keys, switching accounts, restarting Claude sessions, checking credits, and touring a feature-packed dashboard where some functions work and others don't.
Meetup demo of a digital wallet — creating new wallets, checking seeds, using fake wallets and five-cent test tokens to understand how the token flow works before production.

Cafe session wiring Claude agents together through a chat room — cloning a new repo from the same author into upstream repos, working the end-to-end agent lifecycle, needing an API key, and trying Claude in Chrome with website templates and plugins.
One-line thought: bake test coverage in to make life easier when building an extension.

Extension architecture direction: iframe technique for third-party extensions, one GitHub repo per extension (under a future org like kyber-extensions/kybernesis), package.json pointing at the agent SDK for host interface contracts, dynamically loadable into the KyberAgent system with authorization handled later.

actions.text: Adopt iframe-based, dynamically-loaded extensions with one GitHub repo per extension and package.json pointing at the SDK for host interface contracts
Decide the GitHub organization name for extension repos (appydave-kyber / kybernesis / kyber-extensions)
